summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Major Complex Financial Proceedings List – De facto relationship of over 20 years – Alteration of property interests pursuant to s 90SM – Where the parties largely agree on the form of order with the exception of a cash adjustment which requires an assessment of ss 90SM(4) and 90SF(3) factors

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

INTRODUCTION

These de facto property proceedings were commenced by Ms Audrius (now aged 57 years who I will call “the wife”) in November 2023. The orders the wife seeks are opposed by Mr Khamphet (now aged 77 years and who I will call “the husband”).
These two highly qualified health professionals, with the benefit of their legal teams, were able to mostly resolve what constitutes the pool of interests to be divided. However, because of their perceptions of the history of their relationship, which commenced with cohabitation in 1999, and continued until approximately February 2022 (a span of over 20 years), and despite genuine endeavours to do so, they were unable to resolve the final allocation of the pool of interests exceeding over $46 million.
The reasons which follow seek to deal with the parties’ contentions – delivered by experienced Counsel Mr Puckey of King’s Counsel for the wife and Dr Ingleby for the husband, so as to illuminate the pathway to the Court’s ultimate decision.

STATUTORY PATHWAY

The amendments to the property proceedings pathway which came into effect on 10 June 2025 do not apply as the hearing took place in September 2024 when the judgment was reserved.
Accordingly, the pathway can be concisely described.
Shortly stated, but more concisely and elaborately described in the Full Court decision in Hickey & Hickey [2003] FamCA 395; (2003) FLC 93-143 (dealing with a married couple). In a property settlement case, the Court must adopt a well-known four-step process, essentially (for de facto property applications):
(a) to identify the pool of assets and liabilities generally and the legal and equitable interest, and usually at the time of hearing;

(b) to assess the relative contributions of both the financial, non-financial, direct and indirect nature as specified by s 90SM(4) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”);

(c) to consider the factors as are relevant contained in s 90SF(3) of the Act; and

(d) finally, consider the ultimate analysis to determine whether the order the Court proposes to make is just and equitable to both parties.

In circumstances where both parties urged it was just and equitable to make an order (s 90SM(2) of the Act) for the clear reason that the unstated and existing arrangements at separation have altered, I agree to proceed (see Stanford & Stanford (2012) [2012] HCA 52).

CONTEXTUAL HISTORY

Statements of fact hereafter should be construed as findings of fact.
Neither Counsel sought to suggest that the other party was either an unreliable historian or a witness without credit. In my view, no such submission was available, and much of the differences can be seen as founded by the parties perceptions of what they brought into the relationship and what they did (financially and otherwise) during the relationship.
Although it seems some discoverable documents might have been produced only months before the trial, the facts were not significantly in dispute.
At this early stage of these Reasons, it is worth reminding the parties that I am exercising a wide discretion, where the myriad of contributions are to be assessed holistically over the entirety of the of the relationship (see Dickons & Dickons [2012] FamCAFC 154; (2012) 50 Fam LR 244 and Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78). Authorities make clear the assessment of contributions be they income or capital or with more difficultly, non-financial contributions as a homemaker and parent is not a mathematical exercise (see for example Cosio & Cosio (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1A 149). To the extent, but to give context, I record the evidence with some exactness (particularly income earnt). is not to be seen as a failure to recognise the principles I have just stated. Perhaps understandably, where it is accepted that during the long relationship the husband generated greater income than the wife, his desire for a recognition of that differential meant his submissions were shaped by his views. I return to this issue more completely when discussing contributions below.
These parties have generated a sizeable pool, not just through their income as health professionals but through prudent – one might even say highly commercial focussed – purchases and sale of real estate, which has generated significant capital gains. That a number of purchases were made (and properties are still retained) along one of the Region S’s most sought after streets of B Street at Suburb C, demonstrates the parties acumen. That approximately $32 million of the pool of interests (of approximately $46 million) is attributable to property interests held in B Street, demonstrate the point.
Where the current major asset interests are agreed (see Exhibit 2), little is served in these Reasons to record every transaction – rather I will try and record how the current pool was sourced through earlier decisions of the parties – some individually, and many jointly.
The parties are now aged 57 and 77 years respectively, but relevantly were aged approximately 31 and 51 years when cohabitation commenced in 1999. At that time, both were health professionals but the husband had a thriving career shaped over his many years of practice, arriving in Region S in 1981, whilst the wife was a junior professional who attained her degree in 1991 – both practicing the same specialty.
Before cohabitation (or shortly thereafter):
(a) the husband had been in a marriage that produced four (now adult) children, which ended some time before final property Orders were made by the Family Court of Australia (as it then was) in October 2000;

(b) the wife and her former husband Mr U were blessed with the birth of their daughter Ms V in 1996 (now aged 29 years). Again, property settlement was finalised between the wife and her previous partner, she says with Orders made on 7 July 2000;

(c) the wife’s father established a Trust called the K Trust in September 1999;

(d) as I discuss later in these Reasons, the wife and her extended family engaged in a number of property transactions between approximately 1987 and 1996, including acquiring two properties at Suburb W and a property at 3 B Street;

(e) the husband and his ex-wife, similarly, were owners of property, in particular a property at Suburb T (sold in 1998) and at 1 B Street.

With the parties both working in the health profession in Region S, they decided (I find at the husband’s suggestion primarily) to establish in February 2000 an entity called M Pty Ltd (“M Pty Ltd”) which became the vehicle through which their generated income was deposited. Much attention during the hearing was devoted to seeking to quantify the respective incomes generated.
The parties’ family was expanded by the birth of their daughter Ms X in 2001. That child is now an adult aged 24 years.
During 2000, both parties engaged in transactions to fulfil their obligations under the Court Orders with their earlier partners, which included the wife receiving a further 1/6th interest in 3 B Street and the husband selling a property at 1 B Street for $1.8 million when, after clearance of a debt, a payment was received by both parties (see Order 3), with the husband receiving approximately $353,914.
Finalising their prior relationship arrangements allowed the parties (at least from February 2000) to engage in further property transactions, which for context I record (and it is not seriously challenged) included:
(a) the husband purchased 4 B Street for $840,000, with fully borrowed funds from ANZ Bank and where the wife was a guarantor;

(b) the wife paid her ex-husband $110,000 in July 2000 to receive a transfer of his 1/6th interest in 3 B Street – then holding a 1/3rd interest, with her family holding a 2/3rd interest;

(c) during 2001/2002, a new home was constructed on 4 B Street for approximately $1.2 million;

(d) in mid-2001, the parties jointly acquired 7 B Street for $1.2 million and the property was used as a rental property;

(e) in mid-2001, the wife purchased from her grandmother the property at 2 B Street for $2 million. This is the property where the dispute with R Pty Ltd about the construction of a wall arises;

(f) in 2002, the husband purchased a property at Y Street, Suburb Z for $940,000, with the property then renovated and tenanted;

(g) in late 2003, the wife negotiated with her brother Mr AA to acquire his interest (1/6th) in the property at 3 B Street for $400,000. After that transaction was effected, the wife held a 50% share in the property;

(h) During 2004/2005, the husband purchased five subdivided lots as a parcel at CC Street, Town D with one lot (lot ...) subsequently sold for approximately the same amount as the construction and other costs (see paragraph 108 and 109 of the husband’s affidavit);

(i) in September 2005, the wife negotiated with her brother to sell to him her interest in 3 B Street for $1.5 million. There is a dispute between the parties as to whether the wife deposited, as she asserts, $800,000 (or as the husband asserts $603,000) for the proceeds she received into the loan account secured over 4 B Street to discharge the mortgage (which records reveal was not formally released until 2014);

(j) in March 2006, the wife became the Trustee of the K Trust after the wife says she used some of her funds from the sale of her interest in 3 B Street to discharge a mortgage on the Trust property. The husband says (at paragraph 98) that his repayments to the wife of the “loan” of $603,000 was the source to discharge the debt;

(k) in late 2006, the husband sold the Y Street property, for $1,435,000;

(l) shortly after that sale the husband purchased a farm at E Street, Town D for $2,245,389 via the Khamphet Family Trust (No 2), by it seems loaning funds to the Trust (which is included in the balance sheet);

(m) although the reasons for doing so are disputed, in 2015 the property at 4 B Street was transferred into joint names, with the mortgage then registered released around the same time. The husband says he did so to satisfy the wife’s complaint he still owed her money (which he denied), however little turns on the reasons. I note around this time the parties registered their union as a “civil partnership”;

(n) in late 2015, the wife and her parents sold DD Street, Suburb W for $1.1 million, with the wife receiving approximately $600,000;

(o) the wife says that of the funds she received from the sale of the DD Street property, she contributed $300,000 to reduce outstanding loans owing on other properties in their family portfolio;

(p) through a new corporate entity controlled by the husband, he purchased a commercial premises at EE Street, Suburb BB for $693,000 in 2016. This is the premises from which the business – providing management services to other health professionals – was conducted. The husband says the purchase was funded by a loan from M Pty Ltd to his self-managed superannuation fund (SMSF);

(q) in early 2017, the wife and her mother sold FF Street, Suburb W, with the wife receiving approximately $600,000, which again she says, were used to reduce the loans over other properties in the portfolio;

(r) in 2019, the wife’s father passed away with one outcome being that the wife inherited an interest in 8 B Street – now retaining a 50% interest with her mother, which is included in the balance sheet;

(s) in mid-2019, the husband sold 1 CC Street for $975,000;

(t) in mid-2021, the parties decided to sell 7 B Street for $4,750,000, with the nett funds deposited to the wife’s bank account, to fund the construction on 2 B Street by R Pty Ltd (see paragraph 92 of the wife’s affidavit filed 26 August 2024). The husband gives a different version of the use of the funds (at paragraphs 102 and 103 of his affidavit filed 2 September 2024), claiming his “half” of the proceeds was given to the wife as “a loan” and she was to acknowledge the transaction in her will;

(u) the wife says separation occurred in February 2022, when she moved to the home she owns with her mother and that after separation: -

(i) the husband sold 2 CC Street for $1,180,000;

(ii) the husband sold the business N Pty Ltd for $250,000;

(iii) in September 2023, M Pty Ltd paid the husband a dividend of $3 million;

(iv) in early 2024, the husband’s entity sold the Suburb BB commercial property for $863,000;

(v) in mid-2024 the husband purchased a property at G Street, City F for $1.2 million; and

(vi) in June 2024, the wife engaged KK Pty Ltd to commence construction works on 6 B Street. It is those works which are the source of a dispute referred to later.

Whilst I believe I have accurately set out the numerous transactions, I am comforted by the quality of these parties’ legal representation and the extensive discovery that has been undertaken, in being satisfied that the “tracing” of funds through these transactions involving a number of different entities has been undertaken.
To that end, no cross-examination was seriously directed to seek to establish either the wasting of funds or the hiding of funds or assets. Absent a serious allegation of this kind (and where no evidence of any audit being undertaken was offered to the Court), that intermingling of funds occurred is both apparent and likely especially for living expenses. These parties, for almost the entirety of their relationship until final separation, seemed to trust each other enough to have an understanding of various transactions undertaken by the other party, without believing they were undertaken to do anything other than build a solid and strong foundation for their family and their future as a couple.
For these reasons, I am not persuaded about the need to analyse where funds received from various transactions were deposited or used. This includes the dispute, for example, of how much was “lent” to the husband by the wife when she sold 3 B Street or whether, as the husband asserts, the same was repaid.
I also note that apart from the evidence contained in the affidavit of Mr HH (the wife’s accountant since 2008) in respect of potential capital gains liabilities should the wife sell properties at 2 B Street, her interest in 8 B Street and 5 B Street (a subject referred to later), I assume that where no current tax liabilities are included in the balance sheet, any capital gains taxes accrued when interests in real property were previously sold, have all been paid and accounted for within the various entities. The Court is entirely unaware of the extent to which capital gains taxes have in effect reduced any sale proceeds received – or for that matter, the extent to which, if at all, negative gearing of investment properties have attracted some income tax deductions.
Before I deal with the pool of interests, mostly agreed, I will make some findings about what the evidence reveals was income received by the parties (from all sources) during the relationship. It follows from my earlier comments that on the basis of no evidence about taxable capital gains from the sale of real estate (or for example shares), I take the figures offered without further dissection.

INCOME DURING THE RELATIONSHIP

To give context to the findings made in this point of the Reasons, it is agreed that the husband had an established practice as a senior health professional when cohabitation commenced. For example, the husband deposes to earning approximately $975,047 per annum in 1999 (paragraph 50). The wife was a junior health professional and says, and I accept, at the commencement of the relationship she was receiving approximately $350,000 per annum.
In February 2000, the parties “joined forces” by establishing M Pty Ltd, where income from the wife’s practice and the husband’s practice (he was a Director at JJ Hospital and ran units at two private hospitals) were combined.
To the wife’s credit, her evidence reflected not only her admiration for the husband’s entrepreneurial spirit, but also his mentorship and support for her as a more junior health professional. This most clearly became evident when the wife was diagnosed with cancer in 2009 requiring surgery in early 2010.
When M Pty Ltd was established, a system was implemented where M Pty Ltd would coordinate and manage the billing for other health professionals in the group, in return for M Pty Ltd obtaining a “billing fee”. When the wife was recovering from cancer surgery, she received income protection insurance of around $55,000 per month.
At paragraph 68 of her trial affidavit, the wife sets out (she says from her income tax returns) her taxable income for the years 2010 to 2021 as follows:
Year ended

$8,147,433
The wife was not seriously challenged on this figure – which I note is gross/taxable income not nett income and does not assist me by indicating whether the increase, for example in the 30 June 2017 year relates to a taxable capital gain.
No doubt alert, through negotiation, to the wife’s reluctance to accept the husband’s income was substantially superior, the husband at paragraph 88 of his affidavit sets out a “table” of comparative taxable incomes, from all sources (personal, company and trust) which for the same period as revealed by the wife reveals:
Year ended

$1,342,577

$17,100,985
The wife in cross-examination accepted the husband’s evidence at paragraph 88 as accurate. The bare figures speak for themselves. Without any comparative figures for the years from 2000 to 2010, no purpose is served by looking to demonstrate a comparison with the wife when on the figures for those nine years his average taxable income was $1,221,558 ($10,994,025 ÷ 9).
How this difference should, if at all, shape the exercise of my discretion, will be further discussed below.

POOL OF INTERESTS

Exhibit 2, being the updated balance sheet, reveals agreement on all items (and their value) save for:
(a) Item 31 – debt owed to R Pty Ltd of $188,828. In respect of that claim by the wife at the time of the hearing, the wife says she was contesting the claim and had brought a counter claim. The wife failed to produce sufficient evidence to enable this Court to make any assessment of the potential liability, if any, but sensibly, Counsel for the husband, in final submissions, said he could not argue against a sum of $200,000 being allowed to cover the claim or the costs – to be released when the outcome of the dispute is resolved; and

(b) Item 32 – alleged debt to KK Pty Ltd for $150,000, this seems to be the wife’s asserted balance in respect of renovations said to be incomplete, relating to the works on 8 B Street (a property jointly owned with her mother). I accept the submission of Counsel for the husband that the wife carried an evidentiary forensic burden to establish the costs, both before this Court or at least for consideration by the single expert valuer. She did not. I see no injustice in ignoring this claim – which, where it represents less than .3% of the pool, is in my view de minimis.

At Appendix One to these Reasons is the pool of interests, noting that:
(a) the Court was not invited to consider any financial statements for any entities, but that the notes to Exhibit 2 reveal various source documents;

(b) the real properties were, as directed, the result of single expert valuations – although most were undertaken in mid-2024 some 18 months ago;

(c) the parties agreed on how adjustments for loans and/or UPE should be reflected in the balance sheet, such that the Court has not been asked to assess those matters; and

(d) the parties’ entitlements to a SMSF – being item 29 for the wife of $1,480,563 and item 30 for the husband of $2,402,827 are adopted by reason of the parties’ consent (and the way they ran their case), without any financial statements being produced to verify the party is the only member or that no compliance issues exist.

Finally, in noting with a degree of regret which I express that these Reasons have been published with a delay that is not acceptable, neither party had sought leave to re-open the proceedings to seek to adduce any evidence of a different property values or bank balance for the various items. In these circumstances, I have proceeded on the basis of Exhibit 2.

CONTRIBUTIONS

I rely upon the findings previously made in these Reasons, most of which are uncontroversial. Both experienced Counsel, in their oral submissions, tended to emphasise the areas in dispute and their position, consistent with the case outlines filed:
(a) by the wife on 11 September 2024; and

(b) by the husband on 11 September 2024.

On the evidence, and after consideration of the oral and written submissions, I make the following further findings:
(a) The wife’s initial contributions are not disputed by the husband as she sets them out in her affidavit and which are summarised at paragraph 21 of her case outline. I agree that is accurate however in the absence of any retrospective valuation evidence, it is not possible in a monetary sense to calculate the value at the time of cohabitation of those interests. The wife, in addition, had an interest in the K Trust which had been established by her father, with the significant asset being 5 B Street. I am satisfied this Trust property acquisition was funded in 1999 by the wife’s father. I am not satisfied the husband made a contribution of $300,000 as he asserts, noting at the time he was in the middle of a property settlement resolution with his first wife. The K Trust property, the wife accepts, now has a value of $7,300,000 – again demonstrating the capital gain appreciation of real estate in B Street. It is included in the pool of interests as an asset of the wife and should be regarded as a contribution by the wife solely, on the evidence;

(b) The husband’s initial contributions were, in my assessment, significantly less than those of the wife – in part as a result of his property settlement with his former wife, leaving him with a modest return after the sale of their property at 1 B Street. He also had approximately $900,000 in superannuation and some bank accounts. I do not ignore that he also came into the relationship with a far superior earning capacity, as set out earlier in these Reasons – which allowed him to “start again” developing his own property portfolio, with the wife’s support, after his first property settlement;

(c) The benefits the wife accrued from the property interests developed with her parents continued to allow the wife to make direct and indirect financial contributions during the relationship, including:

(i) using the proceeds received from the sale of the DD Street and FF Street properties to reduce debt and increase her equity in 8 and 5 B Street;

(ii) using funds received from the sale of her interest in 3 B Street to do likewise; and

(iii) being able to offer her other property interests as collateral security for the purchase of 7 B Street.

(d) The wife did purchase 2 B Street for $2 million from her grandmother (with a delayed settlement), but despite attempts to demonstrate by mere submission that it was purchased under market value, no probative evidence establishes the wife’s assertion;

(e) The wife says she solely paid for the mortgage repayments, outgoings and expenses for 2 B Street from her own income, however I find, similarly, the husband funded and organised his property acquisitions using his income and sale proceeds. Whilst the parties did maintain separate bank accounts, I find that this couple used each other’s incomes (such as was apportioned to them through the vehicle M Pty Ltd) to build their joint wealth – not to create totally separate silos of wealth, quainted in some way from the other party. Frankly, there was more than enough available income to live as they wished and to make choices about investments, to create wealth. I accept, with the wife’s direct income being significantly less than the husband, the fact that most of her interests in the property portfolio she shared with her family were maintained by payments by her parents (or from rentals etc) meant the wife was getting a continuing benefit from the capital improvement in those assets without having to expend any significant parts of her personal income, to maintain those interests;

(f) The inheritance by the wife of an interest in 8 B Street upon the passing of her father in 2019 was another unmatched contribution – manifested by the increase in value of that current interest in 8 B Street;

(g) In respect of non-financial contributions and contributions as a homemaker and parent, I find:

(i) the wife’s contributions were superior as homemaker and parent although not significantly when one considers the long (and often irregular) hours both parents were required to be available for work. The husband’s increased income reflects, I infer, not only longer hours, but his seniority as well as the specialised services in both managing M Pty Ltd and the heavy responsibilities as a Director. I do not ignore that Ms V was about four years old when this couple commenced cohabitation, and the wife generously conceded that the husband treated Ms V like his own daughter and, in effect, also financially supported her;

(ii) I readily accept that with two hard working parents with a four-year-old child, who was joined by Ms X in 2001, assistance to the household was beneficial. The wife’s parents lived in the same street and were, it seems, both happy and available to assist the family. Such assistance to working parents is neither unusual or unexpected – such is the joy that a grandparent can obtain from a close and loving relationship with their grandchild. What in my experience is unusual, is where a parent seeks to quantify and calculate (without any direct evidence from the wife’s parents) that assistance. The wife’s claim that the grandparents provided 17,575 hours of care, which saved the family up to $800,000 (inclusive of superannuation, leave etc), is both uncorroborated and lacks, other than the admiration of taking the time to estimate the hours, any reliable foundation. I am sure the grandparents were not seeking payment and, are likely, like most grandparents to have assisted where possible. There is no evidence that the parents sought to engage any commercial arrangements – as they were certainly able to afford on their combined income – such as an “in house nanny” or the like. There is evidence that “[Mr LL]” was employed by M Pty Ltd to provide “butler” services including transportation for the parents in some way. Whilst acknowledging the benefit that Ms V and Ms X would have received from doting and capable grandparent involvement, on the evidence I do not regard the generous assistance of the grandparents as a significant non-financial contribution, for which the wife is entitled to assert weighting in her favour.

CONCLUSION ON CONTRIBUTIONS

In the written case outline at paragraph 36, to support the proposal that contribution based entitlements should be adjusted as to 60% to the wife and 40% to the husband, it is contended that in respect of financial contributions:
It is undeniable that the parties’ wealth has been generated through the initial contributions by the Applicant, gifts and inheritances from the Applicant’s parents and the purchase of additional properties during their relationship financed by using the Applicant’s property portfolio as cross-collateral security for the loans. The parties have used their high incomes to fund their lifestyle and to service some of their properties’ expenses (excluding the properties which the Applicant’s parents serviced). On this basis, it submitted that significant weight should be placed on the Applicant’s financial contributions to the relationship.
The husband’s conclusion as to contributions is expressed in a manner which I find “interesting”, and somewhat reflective of the tenor of the husband’s case overall and his evidence and, in some ways, shaped by thinking of his generation (noting the husband is 20 years older than the wife). I think it useful to incorporate in these Reasons paragraphs 4 to 9 of the husband’s case outline, which is said to support and explain the contention that the Court should reject any “loading” based on the wife’s initial contribution, and that a 50/50 finding is appropriate, namely:
First, on the numbers, it is unclear whether she can establish such an imbalance. There are no retrospective valuations.
Second, even if there were greater capital contribution by the applicant, the respondent had a greater earning capacity at that time and earned more money during the relationship than the applicant did. It would be capricious to privilege one amount of money above the other in these circumstances.
Third, the Full Court and its successor have consistently emphasised the importance of a holistic approach to the myriad of contributions made by each of the parties – as to which there is an abundance of evidence in this proceeding.
In a Court which respects the principles of gender neutrality, it is difficult to see why the applicant should be able to succeed on a contention which a male respondent would probably dare not advance in the factual matrix of this proceeding.
More realistically, the contribution-based factors favour the respondent, not solely by relevance to his income but by reference to the extent to which he improved and maintained the applicant’s earning capacity.
This proposition is not put so as to demean the applicant’s qualifications and expertise, rather to assert that, at crucial stages of her career, namely the start and during the period of her illness, he was the one who was there to make sure, and did make sure, that her earning capacity was advanced. He also made contributions to the care and support of the applicant’s child, [Ms V].
These competing perspectives amply demonstrate why, when the pool of interests of $46 million was agreed, the matter could not settle. Both parties could afford and wanted their day in court.
I return to the comment made earlier that this is a holistic exercise, not a mathematical exercise, when I find in broad terms that:
(a) the wife’s initial contributions and the benefits the asset pool received arising from the sale of those interests, maintaining those interests (with her parents’ help) and building on that foundation, together with the late inheritance, are superior to those of the husband and, in my assessment, compel some weighting in the wife’s favour;

(b) I do not ignore the husband’s support for the wife’s career development; assistance through the difficult time of the wife’s diagnosis and surgery, and the fact that his income was significantly greater than that earnt by the wife. Whether the husband’s entrepreneurial flair resulted in a significant income increase is difficult to determine on the evidence. However, those issues, in my assessment, reflect one significant factor within the myriad of contributions and does, in my view, cause the disparity in initial contributions by the wife to be reduced in weight.

I am conscious that in a pool of $46 million, even 1% represents a differential of approximately $460,000.
I find the contribution based entitlements of the parties to be 56% to the wife and 44% to the husband – a differential of 12% or, on the pool of $46 million, a sum of $5,520,000.

SECTION 90SF FACTORS

The disparity in age of the parties has already been noted, and the decision to retire made by the husband was not challenged by the wife.
The wife says, as is abundantly clear, she does not need to continue to work and may decide to retire. However, I infer, as I will discuss next, that decision is likely to be shaped by the orders the Court makes.
This is a case where:
(a) current health issues do not arise for consideration;

(b) the wife has an earning capacity, and could continue to earn an income, for at least the next 10 to 15 years. In saying so, I do not ignore the stressful nature of the service she provides. Her Financial Statement filed 26 August 2024 records a gross salary of $920,608 per annum ($17,704 per week) and with adult children and no other impediments on her working apparent on the evidence, her future available income is a factor which requires some adjustment in favour of the husband;

(c) the wife says she had additional income in the form of interest ($4,079 per week - $212,108 per annum) and pays income tax of $10,201 per week. Part N was not completed (understandably) sp how the wife spends her nett income of around $500,000 per annum is uncertain;

(d) the husband indicated that his current income of $10,200 per week (gross) or $530,000 per annum attracts income tax of $4,700 per week or $244,400 per annum. Similarly, the husband’s use of his nett income for living expenses is uncertain;

(e) whilst each party has superannuation entitlements, the husband can, because of his age, access his entitlements now, tax free, whilst the wife’s ability to access her superannuation (whether by a transition to retirement pension or otherwise), is more restricted; and

(f) I note both parties say they support financially the needs of the children Ms V and Ms X, although they have no legal duty to do so, as the children are adults.

The husband contends that an adjustment of 7.5% to the husband for s 90SF factors is appropriate – the equivalent to the wife paying to the husband a sum of $3,450,000 on a pool of $46 million.
I regard such an adjustment as too high. I assess the contribution based entitlements should be adjusted in favour of the husband by 2.5% – a differential of 5% or $2,300,000.

WHAT ORDERS ACHIEVE JUSTICE AND EQUITY?

It is of course not the percentages discussed above that must be considered to determine whether justice and equity to both parties is achieved, but the nature of the orders.
In this case, what each party shall retain is not in issue, and the orders were similar in form – save for the uncertainty as to whether the wife should make an adjustment to the husband or that the husband should make an adjustment to the wife. The Orders will provide for the wife to establish a fund of $200,000 to meet any expenses ultimately payable, if any, in respect of the R Pty Ltd dispute.
Based on the identified interests in Appendix One to these Reasons, I estimate, before alteration, the parties’ interests are as follows, and on the basis that the husband receives a transfer of the wife’s interest in 4 B Street, namely:
Wife
	
$26,777,191

Husband
	
$19,410,585

Total
	
$46,187,776
If the wife was to receive 53.5% of the pool of interests, which amounts to $24,710,460, she would need to pay the husband $2,066,731.
The husband’s entitlement of 46.5% of the pool of interests, which amounts to $21,477,316, would be achieved by his retention of interests of $19,410,585 and a payment to him by the wife of around $2,066,731. I will round up the payment to $2,067,000.
The wife has cash funds sufficient to make this payment within 30 days. She does not need to sell anything, so reference to capital gains tax implications if a sale of one of the wife’s properties was required, does not arise (Rosati & Rosati [1998] FamCA 38). In any event, the wife’s long-term holding of properties in B Street (a family tradition it seems, going back to at least her grandmother), strongly suggests the wife has no desire to sell in the foreseeable future.
With the Orders at the commencement of these Reasons (based on the template offered by the wife and marked Exhibit 3), each party will have a secure financial future. They both have options to sell property producing little or no income, and invest funds to attract a return to meet living expenses; if income as a health professional ceases or significantly reduces.
For the reasons set out, in the exercise of my discretion, I find that the Orders at the commencement of these Reasons achieve justice and equity and desirable finality.
